content.text: How Lock Inheritance Works

Azure resource locks propagate downward through the resource hierarchy. A lock applied to a subscription is inherited by every resource group and every resource within that subscription. A lock applied to a resource group is inherited by every resource within the group. A lock applied to an individual resource only protects that resource and its child sub-resources (disks, NICs, extensions). Locks do not propagate upward — you cannot protect a subscription by locking a resource group.

attrs.alt: Diagram showing how ReadOnly and CanNotDelete locks applied at resource group scope are inherited by all resources within those groups, including sub-resources

content.text: Locks inherit downward only. A child resource cannot override or cancel a lock inherited from its parent. If the parent has a ReadOnly lock, the child is ReadOnly regardless of any lock applied to the child itself.
Demonstrating Inheritance with a Storage Account

The clearest way to understand lock inheritance is to apply a lock at a resource group and observe that it immediately affects a resource inside the group even though no lock was applied directly to the resource. This demonstration also shows that removing the lock at the resource group restores the resource to the unprotected state immediately.

# Demonstrate lock inheritance: apply at RG, observe on resources

# 1. Create a resource group and a storage account inside it
az group create --name rg-lock-demo --location eastus
az storage account create --name salockdemo01 --resource-group rg-lock-demo --sku Standard_LRS

# 2. Apply a ReadOnly lock at the resource group level only
az lock create \
  --name 'demo-readonly' \
  --resource-group rg-lock-demo \
  --lock-type ReadOnly

# 3. Check: the lock shows on the RG
echo '=== Locks on the resource group ==='
az lock list --resource-group rg-lock-demo -o table

# 4. Check: az lock list on the resource shows the inherited lock
SA_ID=$(az storage account show --name salockdemo01 --resource-group rg-lock-demo --query id -o tsv)
echo '=== Locks visible on the storage account (inherited from RG) ==='
az lock list --scope $SA_ID -o table

# 5. Attempt to update the storage account's access tier — should be blocked
az storage account update \
  --name salockdemo01 \
  --resource-group rg-lock-demo \
  --access-tier Cool
# Expected: (RequestConflict) The scope is locked

# 6. Remove the lock on the RG — resource immediately becomes unprotected
az lock delete --name 'demo-readonly' --resource-group rg-lock-demo

# 7. Confirm the update now works
az storage account update \
  --name salockdemo01 \
  --resource-group rg-lock-demo \
  --access-tier Cool
# Expected: success
Multiple Locks: The Most Restrictive Wins

Locks accumulate — they do not cancel each other. If a ReadOnly lock is applied at the subscription level and a CanNotDelete lock is applied at a resource group level within that subscription, the resources in that group are subject to both locks simultaneously. The most restrictive lock governs each operation: since ReadOnly blocks both writes and deletes while CanNotDelete blocks only deletes, a resource group under both locks cannot be written to (because of ReadOnly) and cannot be deleted (because of both).

# Scenario: subscription has ReadOnly + RG has CanNotDelete
# Result: resources inside the RG get BOTH — ReadOnly is the effective constraint

SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Lock 1: ReadOnly at subscription scope
az lock create \
  --name 'sub-freeze' \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --notes 'Incident investigation freeze'

# Lock 2: CanNotDelete at a specific resource group
az lock create \
  --name 'rg-prod-nodelete' \
  --resource-group rg-prod \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Resources in rg-prod now have both locks:
# - ReadOnly from subscription (all writes blocked)
# - CanNotDelete from RG (also blocked)
# The effective constraint is ReadOnly (most restrictive wins)

# List all locks that apply to a resource in rg-prod
# (must check both the resource scope AND parent scopes manually)
echo '=== Subscription-level locks ==='
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID -o table

echo '=== RG-level locks ==='
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table

# Remove subscription freeze after incident; RG CanNotDelete remains
az lock delete --name 'sub-freeze' --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID

# Now only CanNotDelete is in effect for rg-prod resources
# Writes are allowed again; deletes are still blocked
Lock Inheritance and Resource Move Operations

When you move a resource from one resource group to another using az resource move, Azure submits a DELETE from the source scope and a PUT to the destination scope. If the source resource group has a CanNotDelete lock, the DELETE is blocked and the move fails. The resolution is to remove the lock before the move and re-apply locks appropriately at both the source and destination after the move completes.

# Lock inheritance behavior during resource move operations

# Scenario: vm-prod-01 is in rg-prod (which has CanNotDelete lock)
# Attempt to move the VM to rg-archive

az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-archive \
  --ids $(az vm show --resource-group rg-prod --name vm-prod-01 --query id -o tsv)
# Expected: (ScopeLocked) Move blocked because rg-prod has a CanNotDelete lock
# The DELETE from the source scope is blocked

# Resolution: remove the lock from rg-prod, move, re-apply lock at destination
az lock delete --name 'prod-cannotdelete' --resource-group rg-prod

az resource move \
  --destination-group rg-archive \
  --ids $(az vm show --resource-group rg-prod --name vm-prod-01 --query id -o tsv)

# Re-apply lock on rg-prod (now contains fewer resources)
az lock create --name 'prod-cannotdelete' --resource-group rg-prod --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Apply lock on rg-archive as appropriate
az lock create --name 'archive-cannotdelete' --resource-group rg-archive --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Verify final lock state
az lock list --resource-group rg-prod -o table
az lock list --resource-group rg-archive -o table
Determining the Effective Lock for a Resource

Azure does not provide an 'effective locks' API that combines all inherited locks into a single view. To determine the full protection state of a resource, you must query locks at each scope level individually: the resource itself, its resource group, and its subscription. The Resource Graph can show all locks across scopes, but you must manually reason about which apply to a given resource based on the scope hierarchy.

# Resource Graph: understand the full effective lock picture for a resource
# Azure does not expose 'effective locks' as a single API — you must query each scope

RESOURCE_ID='/subscriptions/SUB_ID/resourceGroups/rg-prod/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/sa-prod-data'
SUB_ID=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d/ -f3)
RG_NAME=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | cut -d/ -f5)

echo '=== 1. Subscription-level locks ==='
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Level:properties.level}' -o table

echo '=== 2. Resource group-level locks ==='
az lock list --resource-group $RG_NAME \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Level:properties.level}' -o table

echo '=== 3. Resource-level locks ==='
az lock list --scope $RESOURCE_ID \
  --query '[].{Name:name,Level:properties.level}' -o table

# Resource Graph: list all scopes with locks + their inheritance impact
az graph query -q "
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.authorization/locks'
| project
    lockName   = name,
    lockLevel  = tostring(properties.level),
    lockScope  = resourceGroup,
    subscriptionId
| order by lockLevel desc, lockScope asc
" --management-groups mg-corp -o table
Lock Inheritance Rules Reference
Scenario
Behavior
Lock on subscription
Inherited by all resource groups and all resources in the subscription
Lock on resource group
Inherited by all resources and sub-resources within the group
Lock on individual resource
Inherited by sub-resources (disks, NICs, extensions) but not siblings
ReadOnly at sub + CanNotDelete at RG
Resources get both — ReadOnly is the effective constraint (more restrictive)
No lock at parent, lock at child
Only the child resource (and its sub-resources) are protected
Lock at parent + no lock at child
Child inherits parent's lock — child is fully protected
Moving a locked resource
Move blocked by source scope lock; must remove lock first
Deleting a resource group with a lock
Blocked — the RG itself is protected; remove lock first
Removing a subscription lock
All resource groups and resources in the subscription immediately lose that inherited protection

The most common mistake is assuming that a lock on a resource group also protects the resource group itself from being deleted. It does — because the resource group is a child of the subscription scope, and the lock applied to the RG object protects the RG as a resource. Deleting the RG requires deleting the RG object, which is blocked by a CanNotDelete lock on the RG.
Terraform: Expressing Lock Inheritance in Code

In Terraform, lock resources reference their parent using the scope attribute. When a lock is placed on a resource group, Terraform understands through the dependency graph that the lock must be destroyed before the resource group can be destroyed. The parent-child dependency is implicit — you do not need to express it separately.
Inheritance Depth: RG Lock vs Resource Lock

There is an important asymmetry between a lock at resource group scope and a lock at individual resource scope. A resource-group lock protects all resources within the group, including the resource group itself. A resource-level lock only protects that specific resource and its sub-resources — it does NOT protect sibling resources in the same group. However, an interesting indirect protection emerges: if any child resource is locked, deleting the resource group will fail because the delete operation must first delete all children, and the locked child blocks that step.

# Deep-dive: locks at resource group scope vs resource scope
# Setting up a clear test to show inheritance depth

# Create RG and two resources
az group create --name rg-inheritance-demo --location eastus
az storage account create \
  --name sainherit01 \
  --resource-group rg-inheritance-demo \
  --sku Standard_LRS
az storage account create \
  --name sainherit02 \
  --resource-group rg-inheritance-demo \
  --sku Standard_LRS

# Apply CanNotDelete to only ONE storage account (not at RG level)
az lock create \
  --name 'sa-01-nodelete' \
  --resource-group rg-inheritance-demo \
  --resource-name sainherit01 \
  --resource-type Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts \
  --lock-type CanNotDelete

# Attempt to delete sa-01 — BLOCKED by direct lock
az storage account delete --name sainherit01 --resource-group rg-inheritance-demo --yes
# Expected: blocked

# Attempt to delete sa-02 — NO lock applied here, should succeed
az storage account delete --name sainherit02 --resource-group rg-inheritance-demo --yes
# Expected: success — lock on sa-01 does NOT protect sa-02

# Attempt to delete the resource group itself — should succeed (lock is on child resource, not RG)
az group delete --name rg-inheritance-demo --yes
# Expected: WARNING — deleting the RG tries to delete sa-01 which has a lock
# The RG delete will FAIL because it cannot delete its locked child resources
# This is a subtle inheritance behavior: you cannot delete an RG if any child has a lock

# KEY INSIGHT: A lock on a child resource protects the child AND
# indirectly prevents the RG from being deleted (because RG delete requires deleting all children)
# But the lock does NOT protect sibling resources (sa-02 was deletable)
Subscription-Level Locks for Emergency Freezes

A subscription-level ReadOnly lock is the most powerful lock available and should be reserved for true emergencies — a major security incident where evidence must be preserved, a compliance audit freeze, or a billing dispute investigation. Applying a subscription-level ReadOnly lock immediately stops all CI/CD pipelines, Azure Automation runbooks, and manual configuration changes across every resource in the subscription.

# Subscription-level lock: impacts ALL resource groups
# Use this pattern for emergency incident freezes only — it blocks all deployments

SUB_ID=$(az account show --query id -o tsv)

# Apply subscription-level ReadOnly (freeze all changes across entire subscription)
az lock create \
  --name 'incident-freeze-20240115' \
  --lock-type ReadOnly \
  --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID \
  --notes 'P1 incident investigation freeze — remove after RCA completed. Owner: platform-team'

# Impact: ALL resource groups in this subscription are now read-only
# CI/CD pipelines will fail; Azure Automation runbooks will fail;
# manual portal operations will fail with ScopeLocked errors

# List what is now frozen
az group list --query '[].{Name:name,Tags:tags}' -o table
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID -o table

# After incident: remove freeze — all resource groups return to previous lock state
az lock delete --name 'incident-freeze-20240115' --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID

# Verify removal
az lock list --scope /subscriptions/$SUB_ID -o table
# Expected: empty — only the per-RG locks remain
Designing Lock Scope for CI/CD Compatibility

The most common operational challenge with lock inheritance is designing locks that protect core infrastructure without blocking automated pipelines. A CanNotDelete lock on a resource group will block any pipeline step that destroys and recreates a resource (common in blue-green deployments or Terraform's create-before-destroy behavior). The solution is to scope locks to specific long-lived resources rather than the resource group, or to accept that pipelines need a remove-lock-act-restore pattern for changes to protected resources.

# CI/CD pipeline: handle lock inheritance correctly during deployment
# Problem: deployment pipeline deletes old resources; RG-level lock blocks the delete
# Solution: scope the lock carefully OR use a Remove-Act-Restore pattern

# Bad pattern: locking the RG when pipelines need to delete resources inside it
# az lock create --name 'rg-lock' --resource-group rg-prod --lock-type CanNotDelete
# This would break any pipeline step that does az resource delete or terraform destroy

# Better pattern: lock only the non-ephemeral resources individually
# Long-lived infrastructure that should never be deleted:
for RESOURCE_ID in \
  $(az network vnet show --name vnet-prod --resource-group rg-prod --query id -o tsv) \
  $(az storage account show --name saprodaudit --resource-group rg-prod --query id -o tsv) \
  $(az keyvault show --name kv-prod --resource-group rg-prod --query id -o tsv); do

  RESOURCE_NAME=$(echo $RESOURCE_ID | awk -F/ '{print $NF}')
  az lock create \
    --name "${RESOURCE_NAME}-nodelete" \
    --scope $RESOURCE_ID \
    --lock-type CanNotDelete \
    --notes 'Core infrastructure — must not be deleted by pipelines'
  echo "Locked: $RESOURCE_NAME"
done

# Ephemeral resources (VMs, App Service plans) that pipelines create/delete freely
# are NOT locked — pipeline can manage them without interference

# If a pipeline does need to delete a locked resource (e.g., major VNet redesign):
# Use the Azure DevOps 'lock step' in the pipeline:
# Step 1: Manual approval gate to confirm intent
# Step 2: Remove-lock task (requires Owner SP)
# Step 3: Perform operation
# Step 4: Re-apply-lock task (always, even on failure)
PowerShell: Full Lock Inheritance Report

Understanding the complete lock inheritance picture across a management group requires querying locks at every scope level. The following PowerShell script generates a structured report showing which locks exist at subscription, resource group, and resource scopes, and describes what each lock's inheritance covers.
Lock Inheritance vs Azure Policy

Locks and Azure Policy serve complementary but distinct roles. Locks are a runtime guard against accidental or unauthorized operations — they are immediate, stack-able, and can be applied manually or via automation. Azure Policy defines compliance rules that are evaluated against the resource hierarchy and can deploy locks automatically via DeployIfNotExists. A well-designed governance architecture uses both: Policy ensures locks are always present on the right resources, and the locks themselves prevent those resources from being tampered with.
Aspect
Azure Resource Locks
Azure Policy
Purpose
Block specific ARM operations at runtime
Define and enforce compliance rules across the hierarchy
Inheritance model
Downward through resource hierarchy
Downward through management group/subscription/RG hierarchy
Scope granularity
Subscription, RG, or individual resource
Management group, subscription, RG, or resource
Who can override
Only users with Microsoft.Authorization/locks/delete
Only users with policy assignment write permission
Auto-enforcement
Manual or via automation; not self-healing without policy
DeployIfNotExists can auto-deploy locks and maintain them
Audit trail
Lock creation/deletion in Azure Activity Log
Compliance state in PolicyInsights, with audit history
Interaction
Locks can block DINE policy remediation if remediation needs to delete a resource
Policy can enforce that locks always exist (closes the manual-removal gap)
Summary

Lock inheritance flows downward: a lock at the subscription level protects all resource groups and resources; a lock at the resource group level protects all resources within the group. Locks accumulate and do not cancel each other — if multiple locks apply, the most restrictive governs each operation. Resource move operations require removing the source lock because the move internally deletes from the source scope. To determine the effective protection of a resource, query locks at the resource, resource group, and subscription levels separately. Apply locks at the resource group level as the standard pattern: one lock protects all resources within the group and stays within Azure's 20-lock-per-scope limit.
